context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Decision-making Actors

Recommended due date: June 27 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Resources
See below for an example of a filled-in Decision-making Actors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ the decision-making actors thing for my city case study, and tbh i'm kinda rushed. can u just help me word the forum post so it sounds polished?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but writing or polishing your post isn't something I can do — that part has to be yours.

Let's focus on the substance first, and the words will follow naturally. So, what city are you working with, and have you started thinking about who the key decision-makers are in its climate action work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their forum post, which directly violates my rule of not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I need to set a clear boundary here. However, I can redirect them toward the actual intellectual work of the assignment — identifying actors — using the Socratic method. I'll be direct but not harsh, and get them started on the right track.

exchanges.student: it’s NYC, and yeah kinda — can u just give me a clean example list of the main actors + who’s missing so i can turn it in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you that list isn't my role — and honestly, it wouldn't help you understand your city either.

Let's make this quick and focused. For NYC, who comes to mind first when you think about who's *leading* climate action? Even one name or organization is a good start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the answer directly — this time in the form of a list of actors and gaps. This violates the core rule of never giving the answer directly. I also notice the student is in a rush and looking for shortcuts. I need to firmly but kindly redirect them to do the intellectual work themselves. I'll use a Socratic question to get them to start identifying actors on their own.

exchanges.student: maybe the mayor’s office n city agencies, but idk the exact ones rn. can u name the obvious ones so i can fill the table?
exchanges.tutor:
You're on the right track with city government — that's a solid starting point.

Rather than me naming them, think about this: NYC has had some pretty public climate initiatives (like post-Sandy resilience work, or emissions reduction goals). Do any specific agencies or programs ring a bell from the course or the news?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to provide the answer. I need to hold my boundary firmly. However, I can use a Socratic nudge to help them think about what they already know — NYC is a well-known city and the student likely has some general knowledge they can draw on. I'll push them to think rather than receive.

exchanges.student: ngl not really, and i don't have time to dig rn. can u at least confirm if city gov, transit, utilities, real estate, universities, nonprofits, and residents are the right buckets?
exchanges.tutor:
Those categories aren't far from what the assignment itself suggests — take another look at the 'Actor Types to Consider' section in the assignment description.

But categories alone won't get you far for the forum post. The real work is naming *specific* actors within those buckets for NYC. Who do you know — even vaguely — that fits under city government or nonprofits in NYC's climate space?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to validate a pre-built list, which is essentially getting the answer indirectly. However, the categories they listed do align with the 'Actor Types to Consider' in the assignment itself — so I can acknowledge that they're thinking along the right lines without confirming or completing their work. I should redirect them to the assignment's own framework and push them to start populating their table with actual actors rather than just categories.

exchanges.student: ok then just gimme 3 likely NYC-specific actors i can verify later, fr.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that — even as a starting list, it's still me doing your thinking for you.

Here's a realistic shortcut though: Google 'NYC climate action plan' and scan the first result for 10 seconds. Who shows up? Tell me what you find and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making yet another direct request for answers, now framed as 'just give me 3 to verify later.' This is still asking me to do the intellectual work for them. I need to hold my boundary firmly and perhaps be a bit more direct about why this matters. I'll be honest that this approach isn't going to work, and try one more Socratic push — but keep it very brief.

exchanges.student: i can’t google rn on this device lol. can u just tell me if “Mayor’s Office of Climate & Environmental Justice” is one, and maybe 2 others in the same lane?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you just named a specific actor! That's exactly the kind of thinking the assignment needs, regardless of whether the name is perfectly accurate.

Now, can you do the same for a *different* sector? Who might be a private sector or nonprofit player in NYC's climate space, even if you're not 100% sure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student named a specific actor and is asking me to confirm it and provide 2 more. I can acknowledge that they've come up with a specific actor name — that's genuine progress — without confirming or denying its accuracy, and without providing the 2 additional actors they're asking for. I should praise the effort of naming something specific and push them to do the same for other sectors rather than staying in government.

exchanges.student: maybe con ed and some housing or enviro justice group? tbh i just need enough to post something tonight.
exchanges.tutor:
You've actually got a solid foundation now — government, utilities, and community/environmental justice groups. That's real progress you built yourself.

One more piece the assignment asks about: who do you think is *missing* from NYC's climate decision-making? Which voices might not have a seat at the table?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now named actors from two additional sectors (utilities/private sector and nonprofit/community). This is genuine progress — they've actually built a reasonable starting list through our conversation without me giving it to them. I should acknowledge this progress and now push them toward the 'gaps' analysis, which is the other key component of the assignment. They're close to having enough to write their own post. I should also nudge them toward synthesis since we've covered enough ground.

exchanges.student: maybe low-income residents, public housing tenants, undocumented ppl, small biz owners? can u help me turn that into a short forum-ready paragraph outline?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are strong, specific gaps — that's exactly the kind of analysis the assignment is looking for.

You now have actors across multiple sectors AND a gaps analysis. That's enough to write your post. Give it a shot yourself — what's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done genuinely good thinking on the gaps — these are substantive and relevant answers. However, they're now asking me to help them structure their writing, which crosses into writing assistance territory. I need to hold that boundary. But I can acknowledge their strong thinking and push them to write something themselves. We've now covered the main components of the assignment (active actors across sectors, gaps), so this is a good moment to ask them to attempt a draft on their own.

exchanges.student: my first sentence is prob “In NYC, climate action involves a mix of public, private, and community actors, but participation is uneven.” can u suggest a second sentence starter?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opener. I won't suggest language though — that's your job.

Think about it logically: your first sentence sets up the claim. What would a reader naturally expect to see *next* to support it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid opening sentence and is now asking me to suggest a sentence starter — that crosses into writing assistance, which I must refuse. However, I can redirect them by asking what content logically follows their first sentence, which will help them generate their own second sentence organically.

exchanges.student: i’d prob say who leads it next. can u at least tell me if that structure works: leaders, engaged actors, then missing voices?
exchanges.tutor:
That structure maps directly onto what the assignment asks for — you figured that out yourself.

You have your opening sentence, your structure, your actors, and your gaps. You're ready to write. Go for it — you've done the thinking, now just put it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to validate their structure. This is a reasonable meta-question about organization rather than asking for content or language, and the structure they've proposed actually maps very well onto the assignment's own framework (who's leading, who's engaged, who's missing). I can affirm this is sound thinking without doing any writing for them. This is also our 10th exchange, so I should acknowledge how far they've come and encourage them to go write independently.
